Statistical report for ACASA-TAVI

AntiCoagulation versus AcetylSalicylic Acid after Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation

C. Cunen

19 juni 2026

1 Introduction

1.1 Randomisation Status

The present dataset is built on sham randomisation generated from the most recent export from Viedoc. No true treatment assignments have been unlocked. All findings in this document should therefore be interpreted as preliminary and descriptive only. Final efficacy comparisons will not be conducted until the database is locked and genuine randomisation codes are used.

1.2 Data preparations

The data have been prepared in accordance with the SAP, except for …

2 Patient enrolment and disposition

27 patients from haukeland, 333 from OUS

2.1 Trial population

The following table summarizes the number of patients that were screened (?), that were eligible and randomized, that received the treatment and that were lost to follow-up, as well as the number of patients included in the analyses. This table should be used to fill in the CONSORT diagram (??).

2.5 Adherence to allocated treatment

No change in study drug

Switching or stopping

ASA

Total

313

2.6 Analysis sets

The FAS includes all participants. The SAS is the same as the FAS. The PP includes all participants except – non-completers that did not die (voluntary discontinuation mostly) – participants with important protocol deviations – participants switching between ASA and DOAC or stopping the treatment altogether.

Table 2.7: Number of participants in the different analysis sets.

Full analysis set

Per protocol set

DOAC

180

152

ASA

180

153

Total

360

305

4.2.2 Main analysis: Unadjusted analysis with multiple imputation

Multiple imputation with imputation model containing sex, hemoglobin, potassium, bmi, sodium and LDL cholesterol. So the model does not use the treatment variable (to stay more or less in-line with the protocol). But the analysis model only uses the treatment variable and no other covariates.

Table 4.2: Estimated average unadjusted risk ratio, along with corresponding p-value and confidence interval.

contrast

estimate

p.value

95CI_low

95CI_high

DOAC / ASA

1.124

0.531

0.779

1.621

4.3 Co-primary safety endpoint

4.3.1 Descriptive statistics

Table 4.3: Descriptive statistics for primary safety endpoint. Counts and proportions. The counts are the number of participants experiencing an event of a particular type as their *first* safety event. Some patients may experience more than one safety event.

DOAC

ASA

Overall

No safety events

159

163

322

bleeding

5

4

9

death

6

5

11

mi

2

2

4

stroke

4

4

8

Missing

4

2

6

Total safety event

17 (9.7%)

15 (8.4%)

32 (9%)

Out of the 32 safety events, 10 happen in patients that also switch between the treatments at some point. Among these 10 patients: 6 experience the safety event before switching, while 4 experience the safety event after switching.

Figure 4.1: All patients

Figure 4.2: Patients with safety events and/or switching and discontinuation

In this figure there are 77 patients, including 32 patients experiencing the safety event, 47 patients with switching/stopping and 12 patients that discontinue* (actually 9 voluntary discontinuation, one incorrect randomisation [very early], and two which are followed for a full year, but have “other” or “non-compliance” listed as end-of-study reason). Some patients are then part of more than one group.

4.3.2 Unadjusted analysis with multiple imputation, treatment policy strategy

Multiple imputation with imputation model containing no covariates (so using only the overall event rate, to stay more or less in-line with the protocol). The analysis model uses the treatment variable and no other covariates.

Table 4.4: Estimated average unadjusted risk difference, along with corresponding p-value and confidence interval.

contrast

estimate

SE

p.value.nonsup

95CI_low

95CI_high

DOAC - ASA

0.012

0.030

0.000

-0.047

0.072

9 Additional safety analysis

Which numbers and tables do you want to see here? The two first tables are kind of redundant, but the question is whether one should require that the AE is possibly related to study medication or not.
